notes.summary: Symmetry is a property of figures where parts repeat in a definite pattern, making the figure look the same from different angles. It can be observed in various objects such as flowers, butterflies, and rangoli designs. Symmetry can be rotational or reflectional.
notes.importance: Understanding symmetry helps us recognize patterns and shapes in nature and art, which is essential for mathematical and scientific concepts.
quick_facts: Symmetry is a property of figures that can be observed from different angles.
There are two types of symmetry: rotational and reflectional.
Rotation through 180° does not result in the original shape for all figures.
The circle has infinite symmetries, including every angle being an angle of symmetry.

notes.summary: Rotational symmetry occurs when a figure looks the same after rotating it by a certain angle. For rotational symmetry to occur, the figure must have at least one line of symmetry that passes through its center. The smallest angle of symmetry is a factor of 360°.
notes.importance: Understanding rotational symmetry helps us recognize patterns and shapes in nature and art, which is essential for mathematical and scientific concepts.
quick_facts: Rotational symmetry occurs when a figure looks the same after rotating it by a certain angle.
The smallest angle of symmetry must be a factor of 360°.
A circle has infinite rotational symmetries.
Some figures may not have rotational symmetry, even after rotating through 360°.

notes.summary: Reflectional symmetry occurs when a figure looks the same on both sides of its line of symmetry. The line of symmetry is an imaginary line that passes through the center of the figure and divides it into two identical parts.
notes.importance: Understanding reflectional symmetry helps us recognize patterns and shapes in nature and art, which is essential for mathematical and scientific concepts.
quick_facts: Reflectional symmetry occurs when a figure looks the same on both sides of its line of symmetry.
The line of symmetry is an imaginary line that passes through the center of the figure.
A circle has infinite reflectional symmetries, including every diameter being a line of symmetry.
Some figures may not have reflectional symmetry.

notes.summary: Angles of symmetry are the angles by which a figure looks the same after rotation. The smallest angle of symmetry is a factor of 360°, and it must be at least 1° for rotational symmetry to occur.
notes.importance: Understanding angles of symmetry helps us recognize patterns and shapes in nature and art, which is essential for mathematical and scientific concepts.
quick_facts: Angles of symmetry are the angles by which a figure looks the same after rotation.
The smallest angle of symmetry must be at least 1°.
The smallest angle of symmetry must be a factor of 360°.
A circle has infinite angles of symmetry, including every angle being an angle of symmetry.
